exercises.title: Basic arithmetic
exercises.scenario: Verify your understanding of basic arithmetic operators.
hints: a - b = 7
10 / 3 = 3 (integer division — drops the decimal)
10 % 3 = 1 (remainder)

exercises.title: Compound assignment
exercises.scenario: Replace verbose assignments with compound operators.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to use compound assignment operators instead of verbose syntax.
hints: x = x + n becomes x += n
x = x - n becomes x -= n
x = x * n becomes x *= n

exercises.title: Comparison operators
hints: 7 < 10 = true
7 == 10 = false (they're different)
7 != 10 = true (yes, they're not equal)
7 >= 7 = true (>= means greater OR equal)

exercises.title: Even or odd checker
exercises.scenario: Build a simple utility to check if numbers are even. This uses the modulo operator — a classic pattern.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `EvenChecker` that:
1. Stores three numbers: 8, 17, 24
2. For each, calculates whether it's even (use modulo)
3. Prints the result

Expected output:
8 is even: true
17 is even: false
24 is even: true

test_cases.expected_stdout:
8 is even: true
17 is even: false
24 is even: true

hints: A number is even if it's divisible by 2 (no remainder)
Use modulo: `n % 2 == 0` checks if n is even
Store each result in a boolean variable for clarity

exercises.title: Precedence challenge
hints: Line 1: * before +, so 3*4=12, then 2+12=14
Line 2: parens first, 2+3=5, then 5*4=20
Line 3: same precedence (- and -), left-to-right: (10-4)-2 = 4
Line 4: same precedence (/ and /), left-to-right: (20/4)/2 = 2
Line 5: * first: 5*2=10. Then left-to-right: 10+10=20, 20-3=17

exercises.title: Average calculator
exercises.scenario: Calculate the average of three test scores. Be careful — this exercise tests integer division.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Average` that:
1. Stores three test scores as int: 85, 92, 78
2. Calculates the average correctly (must include decimal precision)
3. Prints: 'Average: 85.0' (or similar — with decimal)

HINT: If you just do `(a + b + c) / 3` with all ints, you'll get integer division. Cast at least one operand to double.
hints: Sum of int + int + int = int
int / int = int (truncates)
Trick: divide by 3.0 (not 3) — that makes it double division
Alternative: cast the sum: `(double)(score1 + score2 + score3) / 3`

exercises.title: Fix the precedence bug
exercises.scenario: This code is supposed to calculate the average of 4 numbers and check if it's above 70. It has bugs in the expressions.
exercises.instructions:
The code has 2 bugs related to operator usage. Fix them so it produces:
Average: 80.0
Passing: true

bugs.issue: Without parens, only s4/4 happens first (division has higher precedence than addition). And int division loses precision.
bugs.fix: Add parens around the sum, divide by 4.0 instead of 4

bugs.issue: Comparing to 100 — but passing threshold is 70.

hints: Without parens: s1 + s2 + s3 + s4/4 = 70 + 80 + 85 + 21 = 256 (wrong)
The passing threshold should be 70, not 100
Use parens to make the order of operations explicit

exercises.title: Login validator
exercises.scenario: You're writing the validation logic for a login system. A user can log in if they have a valid username AND password AND are not locked out.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `LoginValidator` that:
1. Stores these variables:
   - hasValidUsername (boolean) = true
   - hasCorrectPassword (boolean) = true
   - isLocked (boolean) = false
   - failedAttempts (int) = 2
2. Calculates `canLogin`: true only if all three conditions are good (valid username, correct password, NOT locked)
3. Calculates `shouldWarnUser`: true if failedAttempts >= 2 AND less than 5
4. Prints both results

Expected output:
Can login: true
Should warn: true

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Can login: true
Should warn: true

hints: AND requires ALL conditions true — use &&
NOT (locked) means !isLocked
Range check: x >= a && x < b
failedAttempts is 2, so >= 2 is true AND < 5 is true → result true

exercises.title: Short-circuit evaluation
exercises.scenario: Short-circuit evaluation can be confusing. Verify your understanding.
hints: Line a: both true, result true
Line b: x>100 is FALSE. Java short-circuits — never evaluates y/0 (which would crash). Result false.
Line c: x>0 is TRUE. Java short-circuits — never evaluates y/0. Result true.
Lesson: short-circuit evaluation can prevent crashes

exercises.title: Pre vs post increment
exercises.scenario: The pre vs post increment difference is a classic interview question.
hints: a++ is POST-increment: b gets a's current value (5), THEN a becomes 6
++c is PRE-increment: c becomes 6 FIRST, then d gets that new value (6)
After: a=6 (incremented), b=5 (got old value before increment)
After: c=6 (incremented first), d=6 (got new value)

exercises.title: Time converter
exercises.scenario: Convert total seconds into hours, minutes, and seconds. Real-world use case: video duration, elapsed time.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `TimeConverter` that:
1. Stores totalSeconds = 9045 (just over 2.5 hours)
2. Calculates:
   - hours = totalSeconds / 3600 (use int division)
   - remainingAfterHours = totalSeconds % 3600
   - minutes = remainingAfterHours / 60
   - seconds = remainingAfterHours % 60
3. Prints in format:

9045 seconds = 2 hours, 30 minutes, 45 seconds
hints: This problem is PERFECT for / and % together
Total / 3600 = hours (3600 seconds per hour)
Total % 3600 = remaining seconds after extracting hours
Remaining / 60 = minutes
Remaining % 60 = leftover seconds
Integer division is exactly what you want here

exercises.title: Fix the eligibility check
exercises.scenario: An e-commerce app has a 'free shipping' eligibility check that's broken. Customers are getting wrong results.
exercises.instructions:
Fix the bugs so the code correctly determines free shipping eligibility:
- Free shipping if: order >= 500 AND (member OR first-time buyer)
- For the given values, result should be: true

bugs.issue: Wrong operators. Without parens, && has higher precedence than ||, so it evaluated as: orderTotal >= 500 || (isMember && isFirstTimeBuyer) — wrong logic.
bugs.fix: Use orderTotal >= 500 && (isMember || isFirstTimeBuyer) — both conditions must hold, but the second is satisfied by either being true.

hints: Re-read the rule: order >= 500 AND (member OR first-time)
AND combines the order requirement with the customer-status requirement
OR combines the two customer-status possibilities
Use parens to group: orderTotal >= 500 && (isMember || isFirstTimeBuyer)

exercises.title: Discount calculator with conditions
exercises.scenario: Build a discount calculator. Different discounts apply based on multiple conditions.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `DiscountCalculator` that:

Given:
- Order amount: 1500.0
- Is premium member: true
- Number of previous orders: 5
- Has coupon: false

Calculate boolean variables for each discount type:
- `qualifiesForBulkDiscount`: order >= 1000
- `qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount`: premium AND orders >= 3
- `qualifiesForCouponDiscount`: hasCoupon (just the boolean)
- `qualifiesForAnyDiscount`: any of the above is true

Output exactly:
Order: 1500.0
Bulk discount eligible: true
Loyalty discount eligible: true
Coupon discount eligible: false
Any discount eligible: true

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Order: 1500.0
Bulk discount eligible: true
Loyalty discount eligible: true
Coupon discount eligible: false
Any discount eligible: true

hints: Each discount is a separate boolean variable
Bulk: simple >= check
Loyalty: AND of two conditions (member AND order count)
Coupon: just the existing boolean (no calculation needed)
Any discount: OR of all four — use ||

exercises.title: Complex expression challenge
exercises.scenario: A real interview-level question on operators and precedence.
hints: r3: a>b=true, b>c=true. true && true = true. true || anything = true
r4: !(a<b) = !(false) = true. c!=0 = true. true && true = true

exercises.title: Build a loan eligibility calculator
exercises.scenario: You're building a loan pre-qualification tool. The bank has specific eligibility criteria that combine many conditions.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `LoanCalculator` that determines if someone qualifies for a loan.

ELIGIBILITY RULES:
- Annual income >= 500,000 (5 lakh)
- Age between 21 and 60 (inclusive)
- Credit score >= 650
- Current debt-to-income ratio (debt / income) < 0.4
- Employment status: 'Employed' OR 'Self-Employed'

USE THIS DATA:
- Name: 'Vikram Mehta'
- Annual income: 750000.0
- Age: 32
- Credit score: 720
- Current debt: 200000.0
- Employment status: 'Employed'

CALCULATE AND PRINT:
=========================================
         LOAN PRE-QUALIFICATION
=========================================
Applicant: Vikram Mehta
Annual Income: 750000.0
Age: 32
Credit Score: 720
Debt: 200000.0
Debt-to-Income Ratio: 0.26666666666666666
Employment: Employed
-----------------------------------------
Income OK: true
Age OK: true
Credit OK: true
Debt Ratio OK: true
Employment OK: true
-----------------------------------------
QUALIFIES FOR LOAN: true
=========================================

Note: For employment status, use .equals() since it's a String comparison.

test_cases.expected_stdout:
=========================================
         LOAN PRE-QUALIFICATION
=========================================
Applicant: Vikram Mehta
Annual Income: 750000.0
Age: 32
Credit Score: 720
Debt: 200000.0
Debt-to-Income Ratio: 0.26666666666666666
Employment: Employed
-----------------------------------------
Income OK: true
Age OK: true
Credit OK: true
Debt Ratio OK: true
Employment OK: true
-----------------------------------------
QUALIFIES FOR LOAN: true
=========================================

hints: Break this into smaller calculations — don't try to write one giant expression
Each eligibility rule becomes a separate boolean variable
Use .equals() for the employment status (Strings need .equals)
Age range: x >= 21 && x <= 60
Combine all checks at the end with && for the final qualifies
